observed.surveyMethod: AIMS LTMP manta-tow + photo-transect, GBR-wide sector means
observed.notes: GBR-wide sector average. Northern sector has shown partial recovery (up to 30-33% in some transects) but central and southern sectors remain below the 2014 baseline after five mass bleaching events. The 2024 bleaching event was the most geographically extensive on record.

divingOutlook: The GBR is still the world's largest reef system and a world-class diving destination. Northern sections around the Ribbon Reefs and Coral Sea outliers like Osprey Reef hold the best cover. Expect full reef colour in those areas. Central and southern GBR sectors show more bleaching legacy — seek operator recommendations on which specific sites are performing well.

observed.notes: Cooler-than-average late-summer SSTs in 2024 limited bleaching impact on the Northern GBR's Ribbon Reefs.

divingOutlook: Northern GBR is holding up better than the mid and southern sectors. Expect full reef colour and the resident potato cod. Less crown-of-thorns pressure than further south.

observed.surveyMethod: Reef Life Survey diver fish & benthic transect
observed.notes: Ningaloo experienced sustained thermal stress in early 2024 — extent of mortality varied by reef tract.

divingOutlook: Whale-shark season (Mar–Jul) is the main draw and still excellent. Reef colour is patchy after the 2024 bleaching — focus on the outer reef bommies rather than shallow lagoon.

observed.notes: Raja Ampat retains some of the highest live coral cover in the Coral Triangle. Strong currents and refugia help limit thermal stress.

divingOutlook: One of the few places where what you see today is what divers saw a decade ago. Plan the trip with confidence — peak fish biomass, intact soft-coral fields, manta and wobbegong encounters all reliable.

observed.surveyMethod: Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park photo-quadrat survey
observed.notes: April 2024 surveys recorded localised bleaching on shallow reef flats; submerged reef slopes remained largely unaffected.

divingOutlook: Reef slopes from 10 m down are still vivid. Shallow tops show some bleaching scars. Liveaboard season is short (Mar–Jun) — book early.

observed.notes: Caribbean-wide 2023–2024 heat dome impacted Cozumel; SCTLD (stony coral tissue loss disease) continues to compound losses.

divingOutlook: Less hard coral than the photos. Fish life, sponges, and the wall topography are still excellent. Best at depth (>20 m) and on the cooler shoulder months (Dec–Mar).

observed.notes: Bonaire saw widespread bleaching during the 2023 marine heatwave; recovery is patchy but ongoing.

divingOutlook: Shore diving access is still world-class. The reef is rebuilding — expect smaller coral colonies than older photos. STINAPA conservation fee directly funds recovery, so go and support it.

observed.notes: Fakarava's UNESCO Biosphere status and limited human pressure correlate with stable cover. 2024 SST anomalies were modest.

divingOutlook: South Pass shark wall is still the densest grey-reef-shark aggregation in the Pacific. Reef structure largely unchanged in a decade — what you book is what you get.

observed.notes: Northern Red Sea corals continue to show unusual thermal tolerance — one of the few global refugia.

divingOutlook: One of the rare reefs that has not lost cover in the last decade. Northern Red Sea corals tolerate heat better than most. Expect full colour, sharks at Yolanda, schooling jacks year-round.

observed.notes: West Hawaii reefs were hit by elevated SSTs in late 2024; partial recovery underway through 2025.

divingOutlook: Manta night dive and blackwater are both unchanged — those don't depend on coral. Day diving over reef shows recovery in patches. Worth going for the pelagic experiences alone.

observed.surveyMethod: Palau International Coral Reef Center benthic survey
observed.notes: Palau's deep oceanic upwelling provides some thermal buffering for outer reefs.

divingOutlook: Blue Corner shark current still produces. Outer reefs buffered by oceanic upwelling. Inner lagoon reefs show more bleaching — skip the inside in favour of the channels.

observed.notes: Sipadan benefits from MPA status; bleaching in 2024 was concentrated on shallower terraces.

divingOutlook: Barracuda Point and turtle traffic are unchanged. Deep walls (>15 m) hold the best coral cover. Permit quota limits diver impact — a key reason it's still in this shape.

observed.surveyMethod: Maldives Marine Research Institute reef monitoring
observed.notes: Maldives saw widespread bleaching in 2024 with stronger impact on shallow lagoon reefs than ocean-side thila and kandu.

divingOutlook: Manta and whale-shark encounters at Ari are still the main draw and are not coral-dependent. Hard coral has thinned noticeably since 2014. If you've been waiting on Maldives, this is the year — not later.

observed.notes: Remote Coral Sea atoll (~350 km from GBR) with minimal human pressure. Steep oceanic walls and cooler upwelling buffer thermal stress. Cover has held steady over the decade; shallow terraces show only minor bleaching from the 2024 mass-bleaching event that affected inshore GBR reefs more severely.

divingOutlook: One of Australia's most pristine outer-shelf reefs. Isolation means virtually no sedimentation or nutrient runoff — expect full reef colour, grey reef sharks on the walls, and large pelagics year-round. Better protected than most GBR sites.

observed.notes: Southern Caribbean site off Aruba's northwest coast. Aruba sits outside the main hurricane belt and benefits from cooler upwelling from the Venezuelan coast, moderating SSTs relative to the northern Caribbean. The site's primary draw is the Antilla wreck; surrounding reef shows the moderate decline typical of the southern Caribbean over the past decade, compounded by the 2023 regional heat event.

divingOutlook: The Antilla wreck is the headline dive and is not coral-dependent — encrusted hull sections and resident fish life remain excellent. Living reef around Malmok has thinned since 2014 but Aruba's cooler, calmer conditions mean it fares better than many northern Caribbean sites.

observed.surveyMethod: AGRRA biennial transect surveys + Coral Reef Alliance monitoring + Operation Wallacea research dives; DNA sampling by University of Miami June 2024
observed.notes: 60% is a conservative post-2024-bleaching estimate. Pre-2023 surveys consistently reported 60-70% cover — 68% is the most-cited figure (Coral Reef Alliance, NatGeo, Operation Wallacea). The 2023-2024 global bleaching event caused significant mortality at some sub-sites, while Cocalito and Banco Capiro showed above-average resilience. No peer-reviewed 2024 survey figure published as of June 2026. Heat-tolerant symbiont (clade D zooxanthellae) confirmed in elkhorn corals by University of Miami Baker lab (preliminary, 2024). Diadema sea urchin density 2.5/m2 vs Caribbean average 1/100m2 — this anomalous population survived the 1983 Caribbean-wide pathogen wipeout. 46 coral species, 83 fish species documented. Caribbean average coral cover ~17% (GCRMN 2024).

thermalStress.notes: While Honduras North Coast reached severe alert levels in 2024, Tela's heat-tolerant clade D zooxanthellae symbionts (confirmed University of Miami, 2024) mean the effective bleaching response was significantly below the regional SST anomaly. 60% post-event coral cover confirms this. DHW estimated at watch-level impact despite regional alert-1 conditions.

divingOutlook: Tela Bay holds some of the most intact reefs in the Caribbean. Coral cover above 60 percent — over 3x the regional average. Banco Capiro and Cocalito are the strongest sites. Elkhorn coral colonies here are being studied for heat-tolerant genetics; what you are diving on is living scientific research material.

observed.surveyMethod: Reef health assessment across Wakatobi and Takabonerate parks (multi-site); Line Intercept Transects on Kaledupa and Hoga islands by Operation Wallacea / LIPI (2006-2019)
observed.notes: Park-wide average 29.48% hard coral cover (May 2019 survey, Hamdani et al. BIO Web of Conferences 2024). Highest individual site: Wanci at 45.53%; Kaledupa fringing reef reached 46% in 2024 Ocean Science Journal study. Hard coral cover declined ~20% following the 2010 bleaching event (60% of park corals bleached, <5% mortality) then recovered steadily through 2019. In 2024 during the 4th global bleaching event — when 84% of world reefs experienced bleaching-level heat stress — Wakatobi recorded no significant bleaching, attributed to locally evolved heat tolerance. SST anomalies at Wakatobi are documented not to predict benthic composition changes (PMC9448965, 2022), an anomaly compared to other reef systems.

thermalStress.notes: No bleaching-level alert documented at Wakatobi during the 2024 global bleaching event. Reef scan photo surveys and eDNA sampling confirmed healthy reef condition. Nearest NOAA CRW gauge: Maluku, Indonesia regional product. Wakatobi corals show anomalous heat tolerance — SST anomalies do not predict benthic composition changes here as they do elsewhere.

divingOutlook: Wakatobi sits at the heart of the Coral Triangle with one of the longest continuous reef monitoring records in Indonesia. Coral cover near 30 percent park-wide with individual sites reaching 46 percent. The reef did not bleach in 2024 when most of the world's reefs did. Operation Wallacea runs research expeditions from Hoga Island annually — divers here are diving alongside active science.

observed.surveyMethod: Underwater diving surveys (belt transects and rapid assessments) by Oceana Philippines 22-day expedition; Coral Cay Conservation LIT baseline surveys 2002-2004
observed.notes: Oceana Philippines 2020 expedition found outer Panaon Island reefs at 60-70% live coral cover — approximately 3x the Philippine national average (~20%). Inner bay coastal fringe surveys (CCC 2002-2004) recorded 10-15% cover, indicating strong spatial heterogeneity. The 45% figure used here represents a conservative middle estimate across the full bay. Panaon Island was identified by the 50 Reefs Initiative (Bloomberg Philanthropies) as among the 50 reefs globally most likely to survive climate change. LAMAVE has documented 350+ individual whale sharks at this site over 10+ years. No site-specific bleaching percentage data available for 2023-2024 global bleaching event. Crown-of-Thorns Starfish outbreak documented 2019-2022 (15-53 individuals per reef area).

thermalStress.notes: Philippines broadly experienced Alert Level 1-2 conditions during the 2023-2024 global bleaching event. Panaon Island outer reefs are recognized by the 50 Reefs Initiative as among the most thermally resilient reefs globally. No site-specific Sogod Bay bleaching report published for 2024 as of June 2026. Estimate based on Eastern Philippines regional NOAA CRW gauge.

divingOutlook: The outer reefs around Panaon Island are among the healthiest in the Philippines — coral cover well above the national average and whale shark encounters virtually guaranteed from November through April. This is one of the quietest major aggregation sites in Asia. LAMAVE researchers are regularly in the water and welcome diver observations.

observed.surveyMethod: Underwater Photo Transect (UPT) using GoPro imagery analyzed on CoralNet platform (100 points per image); validated with video-recorded line transects and Sentinel-2 satellite imagery. Surveys conducted December 2022, February 2023, April 2023.
observed.notes: UPT surveys Dec 2022-Apr 2023 found average hard coral cover 32.80% across stations (range 12.8% at Pulau Panjang to 45.9% at Sangalaki Island). Two-decade satellite/ML classification study (Landsat 7 / Sentinel-2 / UAV, MDPI Sensors 2024) shows cover at 31.78% in 2003, declining to 27.42% in 2011 following bleaching events, recovering to 31.68% in 2021. No site-specific bleaching percentage available for 2023-2024 global bleaching event; Coral Triangle broadly impacted. Fish bomb damage, cyanide fishing, and anchor dragging have caused localized acute damage but have not prevented system-level recovery. Marine Megafauna Foundation has documented 155 individual resident reef manta rays; 24% of females observed pregnant — the only known site in East Borneo with regular pregnant reef manta occurrences.

thermalStress.notes: Coral Triangle region experienced active HotSpots >1 degree C during the 4th global bleaching event (2023-2025). Bleaching warning events documented at Derawan in 2002-2003, 2010-2011, 2020, and 2021. No site-specific 2024 bleaching survey published for Derawan as of June 2026. Alert level estimated from NOAA CRW 5km product at 2.25N 118.42E.

divingOutlook: Sangalaki Island is one of a handful of places worldwide with year-round resident reef manta rays — and the only site in East Borneo where pregnant females regularly appear. Hard coral cover has recovered close to 2003 levels after the 2010-2011 bleaching impact. Enforcement patrols with 24/7 radar monitoring began in 2023; seizures and prosecutions for blast fishing increased sharply in 2024.
